ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore economic rice mixed plate; 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singapore economic rice; A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with two vegetable dishes and one fried meat dish typically weighs approximately 400 g as served at a Singapore hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Fried Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Fried Meat), cr_id F05100040. All per-100g values are as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked white rice (high GI, ~72), which forms the dominant carbohydrate base of this dish. The composite dish GI is estimated using white rice as the primary carbohydrate driver; the vegetable and fried meat components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 64.48) / 100 ≈ 46.4, based on 400 g serving with 16.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 254 mg per 100 g (approximately 1016 mg per 400 g serving) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g per 100 g) and saturated fat from fried meat contributing to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request less sauce or gravy, replace the fried meat with a steamed or braised protein option, and increase vegetable intake to improve potassium and fibre offset against sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with 16.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g and low dietary fibre (1.2 g per 100 g) produces a high glycaemic load per serving; fried meat adds excess fat that worsens postprandial insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, substitute brown rice where available, replace the fried meat with steamed fish or braised tofu, and increase the vegetable portion to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried meat and sodium at 254 mg per 100 g elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; the low fibre content (1.2 g per 100 g) provides insufficient cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the fried meat component and substitute with a steamed or grilled protein, request minimal sauce to reduce sodium, and choose a larger proportion of vegetables to improve dietary fibre and micronutrient intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; the fried meat component overrides the anti-inflammatory benefit of the vegetable dishes.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the fried meat with a steamed or braised protein to reduce AGE intake, retain and increase the vegetable dishes, and avoid red meat where possible due to its high arachidonic acid (omega-6) content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (2 Vegetables, 1 Fried Meat), known in Mandarin as 经济饭 (Jīng Jì Fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi, is one of Singapore's most ubiquitous hawker centre meals. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice served alongside two self-selected vegetable dishes — commonly stir-fried leafy greens such as kai lan, cabbage, or long beans — and one fried meat dish, typically deep-fried pork belly, fried chicken, or battered fish. The meal is assembled cafeteria-style at cai fan (菜饭) stalls found throughout Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and coffee shops. The rice is soft and mildly flavoured, the vegetables are lightly seasoned with soy sauce or oyster sauce, and the fried meat contributes a crispy, savoury, and fatty element. Per 100 g, the dish provides 143 kcal, 4.98 g protein, 6.34 g fat, and 16.12 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 254 mg and dietary fibre at only 1.2 g. The high-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with low fibre creates a high glycaemic load, making this meal a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The sodium content and saturated fat from the fried meat are relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is affordable, filling, and culturally central to Singapore's working-class lunch culture, commonly consumed as a weekday midday meal.

Is cai fan good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100040), which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The exact dish is present in the HPB database with a confidence of 0.95. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the primary carbohydrate driver, which is a defensible analogous estimate.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 1.8 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for a similar mixed rice dish with fried meat, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry; this is documented as an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available from a secondary approved source with sufficient specificity for this composite preparation. The dietary_tags in the input ('vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie') have been corrected: this dish contains fried meat and is therefore not vegetarian or plant-based; the low_calorie tag is debatable at 143 kcal per 100 g for a composite dish and has been removed. Halal status is not confirmed as the fried meat component may be pork; is_halal is set to false pending confirmation of meat type. GI glycemic load per serving is calculated on a 400 g serving basis: (72 × 16.12 × 4) / 100 = 46.4.
